name: Kaluza-Klein dark matter
summary: In universal-extra-dimensions (UED) models, all SM fields propagate in compact extra dimensions of size ~ TeV^-1. Momentum conservation in the extra dimensions, broken to a residual KK parity by orbifold boundary conditions, makes the lightest KK mode (LKP - typically the first KK photon B^(1)) absolutely stable. Provides a thermal WIMP DM candidate with mass ~ 1 TeV and unique collider signatures.

addresses_puzzles.note: B^(1) thermal relic abundance reproduces Omega_DM h^2 ~ 0.12 for 1/R ~ 800-1500 GeV; LHC bounds on UED have pushed 1/R > ~1 TeV, partly closing this window. Direct detection bounds (LZ) further constrain.

touches_benchmarks.note: Spin-independent and spin-dependent cross sections from KK quark / KK Higgs exchange are at LZ-saturating levels for TeV-scale 1/R.
B^(1) annihilation to leptons gives a hard positron / gamma-ray signal; constrained by Fermi-LAT dwarf observations.
KK top loops modify Higgs production and decay; LHC Higgs coupling fits constrain UED at 1/R > 1 TeV level.

new_fields: KK towers of all SM fields
new_symmetries: KK parity (residual of momentum conservation in compact dimension)
introduces.note: Compactification scale 1/R, number of extra dimensions, brane-localised kinetic terms (radiative corrections).

history: Servant and Tait (2002), 'Is the lightest Kaluza-Klein particle a viable dark matter candidate?', arXiv:hep-ph/0206071, published Nucl. Phys. B 650 (2003) 391. Subsequent decade of UED phenomenology; LHC squeezed 1/R bound to >~ 1 TeV, narrowing the relic-abundance window.

references.citation: Servant and Tait, 'Is the lightest Kaluza-Klein particle a viable dark matter candidate?', Nucl. Phys. B 650, 391 (2003)
